paper_title: RynnWorld-Teleop: An Action-Conditioned World Model for Digital Teleoperation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Scaling robot learning requires massive, diverse trajectory data, yet collection is currently bottlenecked by physical teleoperation, where every demonstration binds operator time to specific hardware and workspaces. We introduce digital teleoperation, a paradigm that decouples data collection from physical constraints by replacing the real robot with a generative world model. In this framework, an operator's hand-pose stream drives a robot-centric generative world model to synthesize high-fidelity egocentric videos from a single reference image. The recorded pose stream serves as an embodiment-agnostic action label transferable to any target robot via standard retargeting, yielding complete state-action trajectories for imitation learning independent of physical hardware. We instantiate this paradigm in RynnWorld-Teleop, a system that integrates depth-aware skeletal conditioning, progressive human-to-robot training on a video Diffusion Transformer, and streaming autoregressive distillation. This pipeline compresses the generative process into a single-pass inference, enabling 40+ FPS, real-time interactive generation on a single H100 GPU. Policies trained exclusively on RynnWorld-Teleop-generated data achieve effective zero-shot Sim2Real transfer across dexterous and diverse bimanual tasks. Moreover, augmenting real-world datasets with our digitally teleoperated data consistently improves success rates, demonstrating that RynnWorld-Teleop serves as a high-fidelity, scalable data engine for the next generation of robotic agents.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: RynnWorld-Teleop: An Action-Conditioned World Model for Digital Teleoperation Haoyu Zhao1,2,3, Xingyue Zhao1, Hangyu Li5, Biao Gong6, Kehan Li1,4, Siteng Huang1,4, Xin Li1,4, Deli Zhao1, Zhongyu Li2,3 1DAMO Academy, Alibaba Group, 2Hong Kong Embodied AI Lab, 3CUHK, 4Hupan Lab, 5Alibaba Group, 6Ant Group Equal contribution, Corresponding author Scaling robot learning requires massive, diverse trajectory data, yet collection is currently bottlenecked by physical teleoperation, where every demonstration binds operator time to specific hardware and workspaces. We introduce digital teleoperation, paradigm that decouples data collection from physical constraints by replacing the real robot with generative world model. In this framework, an operators hand-pose stream drives robot-centric generative world model to synthesize high-fidelity egocentric videos from single reference image. The recorded pose stream serves as an embodiment-agnostic action label transferable to any target robot via standard retargeting, yielding complete state-action trajectories for imitation learning independent of physical hardware. We instantiate this paradigm in RynnWorld-Teleop, system that integrates depth-aware skeletal conditioning, progressive human-torobot training on video Diffusion Transformer (DiT), and streaming autoregressive distillation. This pipeline compresses the generative process into single-pass inference, enabling 40+ FPS, real-time interactive generation on single H100 GPU. Policies trained exclusively on RynnWorld-Teleopgenerated data achieve effective zero-shot Sim2Real transfer across dexterous and diverse bimanual tasks. Moreover, augmenting real-world datasets with our digitally teleoperated data consistently improves success rates, demonstrating that RynnWorld-Teleop serves as high-fidelity, scalable data engine for the next generation of robotic agents. https://alibaba-damo-academy.github.io/RynnWorld-Teleop.github.io https://github.com/alibaba-damo-academy/RynnWorld-Teleop https://huggingface.co/Alibaba-DAMO-Academy/RynnWorld-Teleop https://www.modelscope.cn/models/DAMO_Academy/RynnWorld-Teleop Date: July 8,

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: Recent advances in robotics, such as vision-language-action (VLA) models (Black et al., 2024; Brohan et al., 2022; Kim et al., 2024; Li et al., 2026b; Zhao et al., 2026b) and world models (Agarwal et al., 2025; Ali et al., 2025), show emerging promise for general-purpose autonomy, yet they remain significantly hindered by data scarcity (Zhao et al., 2026a; Lepert et al., 2025a; Zhao et al., 2023). Access to such large-scale robot data would enable more straightforward training and potentially unlock higher performance upper bound. While traditional teleoperation systems (Li et al., 2025b; Ze et al., 2025; Zhao et al., 2025a) provide high-quality expert data, they are often confined to fixed laboratory settings and specific objects Zhao et al. (2025b); Guo et al. (2026); Zhao et al. (2024). The immense overhead of manual environment resets and the logistical challenge of procuring diverse real-world objects prevent these systems from capturing the long-tail distribution of interactions. Consequently, achieving the robust generalization required for unstructured environments remains an open challenge that physical platforms alone cannot solve. We propose to decouple operator time from physical infrastructure by replacing the real robot with generative one. We call this paradigm digital teleoperation: an operators real-time hand-pose stream is consumed by Figure 1 Physical vs. Digital Teleoperation. (Top) Physical teleoperation binds every demonstration to real robot and fixed workspace, capping throughput at operator-hours hardware availability. (Bottom) Digital teleoperation replaces the real robot with RynnWorld-Teleop, real-time action-conditioned world model that synthesizes the egocentric video the robot would have produced from single reference image, and retargets the same gesture stream into embodiment-specific robot actions. Both pipelines emit synchronized (RGB observations, robotic actions) pairs, so digital teleoperation is drop-in compatible with downstream imitation learning, without ever moving real robot. generative world model that, conditioned on single reference image of the target scene, synthesizes the egocentric video that the robot would have produced if it had actually executed the gesture  (Fig. 1)  . Crucially, because the generated video is grounded in the operators joint-level hand poses, the recorded gesture itself serves as an embodiment-agnostic, ground-truth action label that can be transferred to any concrete robot embodiment via standard retargeting pipeline. The generated video then provides the matching visual observations, and the resulting trajectories can be consumed directly by downstream imitation learning, without ever moving real robot. If digital teleoperation works, robot data collection becomes bound only by operator imagination, not by physical infrastructure. Two threads of recent work touch on this goal but fail to bridge the gap. Human-to-robot video translation methods (Lepert et al., 2025a; Li et al., 2025a; Lepert et al., 2025b; Song et al., 2025) attempt to robotize human videos by rendering or translating robot embodiment into human demonstration frames to bridge the visual gap. While they cross the visual gap, they are inherently passive and observation-only: the underlying robotic action is never produced, and the heavy DiT backbones rule out closed-loop interaction. Action-conditioned egocentric world models (Hao et al., 2026; Wang et al., 2026, 2025b; Xie et al., 2026; Tu et al., 2025) go further by synthesizing future frames from human-controlled signals, but they remain human-centric. The rendered hand is still human hand, leaving the embodiment gap unbridged. genuine digital teleoperation system must simultaneously (i) be robot-centric, so the operator can teleoperate robot; (ii) be action-grounded, so every generated frame is tied to recoverable joint-level action signal; and (iii) be real-time, so the operator stays in the control loop and can string complex skills together. No prior framework satisfies all three. Figure 2 Depth-Aware Representation. We bridge the gap between 2D projections and 3D dynamics by rendering hand skeletons with depth-modulated color and size. To this end, we present RynnWorld-Teleop, the first system that delivers digital teleoperation in this strict sense, as illustrated in Fig. 1. RynnWorld-Teleop is robot-centric, action-conditioned world model that streams high-fidelity interactive video from the egocentric viewpoint at frame rates compatible with real-time control. It is built around three core designs tailored to the requirements above: Depth-aware action representation (Sec. 3.2, Fig. 2): RynnWorld-Teleop renders 21-joint hand poses with camera-distance-modulated color and radius, supplying explicit 3D cues via 2D latent conditioning signal. Progressive cross-domain training (Sec. 3.4): RynnWorld-Teleop is first pretrained on large-scale egocentric human videos to absorb manipulation priors, and then fine-tuned on paired humanrobot data to bridge the embodiment gap. Streaming autoregressive distillation (Sec. 3.5): We distill bidirectional teacher into causal student using rollout-consistent schedule, enabling stable, real-time, long-horizon generation. We evaluate RynnWorld-Teleop both as generative model and, more importantly, as digital teleoperation system whose output is robot training data. Crucially, policies trained exclusively on RynnWorld-Teleopgenerated data achieve effective zero-shot Sim2Real transfer across diverse real-world manipulation tasks, and augmenting real demonstrations with digitally teleoperated data consistently raises success rates. These results demonstrate that RynnWorld-Teleop successfully turns digital teleoperation from an appealing concept into scalable and efficient tool for the next generation of robot learning. In summary, our work makes the following contributions: We formalize digital teleoperation as new paradigm for robot data generation, where operator hand-pose streams drive robot-centric world model to generate videos for imitation learning, and identify the three requirements (robot-centric, action-grounded, real-time) that any practical instantiation must satisfy. We present an action-conditioned egocentric world model RynnWorld-Teleop that meets all three requirements, combining depth-aware skeletal representation, progressive human-to-robot training, and streaming autoregressive distillation into single 40+ FPS interactive model. We further extend RynnWorld-Teleop into complete digital teleoperation system that instantiates an arbitrary manipulation scene from single reference image, generating unbounded action-conditioned rollouts through retargeting, skeletal-conditioned synthesis, and chunked re-anchoring. We show that policies trained purely on data generated by our digital teleoperation system can transfer zero-shot to real robots, and that augmenting real demonstrations with such data further boosts success rates, providing the first empirical evidence that digital teleoperation can serve as high-fidelity data engine that both substitutes for and amplifies physical teleoperation in robot learning.

sections.title: 2.1 Action-Conditioned Egocentric World Models
sections.content: The paradigm of world modeling has recently shifted from latent-space state prediction (Ha and Schmidhuber, 2018; Assran et al., 2025) to high-fidelity visual simulation, powered by the rapid progress of video diffusion models (Kong et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2025a; Yang et al., 2024). Foundation models such as Cosmos (Agarwal et al., 2025) and Genie (Bruce et al., 2024) demonstrate the potential for physical AI by training on massive video datasets. However, these models typically support only coarse-grained controls, which are insufficient for complex robotic tasks. To achieve nuanced control, new class of action-conditioned egocentric world models (Hao et al., 2026; Wang et al., 2026; Xie et al., 2026; Tu et al., 2025; Goswami et al., 2025; Pallotta et al., 2025; Gao et al., 2026) emerge, focusing on generating first-person videos driven by human action signals. For example, Hand2World (Wang et al., 2026) distills (Yin et al., 2025; Huang et al., 2025) bidirectional video diffusion model into causal autoregressive generator for monocular streaming synthesis. GeneratedReality (Xie et al., 2026) conditions future-frame synthesis on hand actions to generate interactive videos. InterDyn (Akkerman et al., 2025) conditions on binary hand masks via ControlNet (Zhang et al., 2023)-like branch, while CosHand (Sudhakar et al., 2024) generates single-frame hand-object interactions from mask inputs. Despite these advancements, existing simulators remain predominantly human-centric, primarily focusing on synthesizing human hand movements within virtual scenes. RynnWorld-Teleop extends this frontier to the robot-centric domain, bridging the gap between large-scale human motion priors and actionable, robot-specific world modeling.

sections.title: 2.2 Human-to-Robot Video Translation
sections.content: Modern video generative models have evolved from text-to-video synthesis (Kong et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2025a; Yang et al., 2024) to complex systems handling multi-modal prompts (Bruce et al., 2024; Jin et al., 2024). Within the robotics domain, early efforts (Kuaishou, 2024; Runway, 2025) focused on visual Human-to-Robot translation. For example, Phantom (Lepert et al., 2025b) and Masquerade (Lepert et al., 2025a) use inpainting and rendering techniques to overlay robot arms onto estimated human poses. X-Humanoid (Yang et al., 2025) and Mitty (Song et al., 2025) leverage Diffusion Transformers for video-to-video translation, while H2R (Li et al., 2025a) proposes human-to-robot data augmentation for pretraining. Although crossing the visual domain gap, these approaches are largely passive and observation-only. They perform visual retargeting on pre-recorded videos without providing mechanism to control future states via actions. This lack of action-conditioning makes them unsuitable for interactive simulators. RynnWorld-Teleop distinguishes itself by shifting from passive visual translation to active, action-conditioned generation, effectively bridging the gap between human motion priors and actionable robotic data.

sections.title: 3 RynnWorld-Teleop: An Action-Conditioned Egocentric World Model
sections.content: Given reference image Iref and hand-gesture sequence = {p1, . . . , pT }, RynnWorld-Teleop synthesizes high-fidelity robotic egocentric videos = {v1, . . . , vT }. To bridge the embodiment gap between human intent and robotic execution, our framework integrates: (i) depth-aware action representation (Sec. 3.2) and the task-specific architecture (Sec. 3.3); (ii) two-stage progressive training paradigm for human-to-robot knowledge transfer (Sec. 3.4); and (iii) an autoregressive distillation process that enables real-time interactive generation (Sec. 3.5).

sections.title: 3.1 Preliminaries
sections.content: RynnWorld-Teleop builds upon the Wan-I2V architecture (Wang et al., 2025a), which utilizes 3D Variational Autoencoder (VAE) and Transformer-based denoiser FΘ. The training objective follows the conditional flow matching (CFM) framework (Lipman et al., 2022). Given an initial image latent z0 = E(IV ), the forward process constructs probability path between data and noise: zt = (1 t)z0 + tϵ, ϵ (0, I) (1) Figure 3 Overview of RynnWorld-Teleop. (a) Actions are rendered as depth-aware skeletal videos and encoded into the latent space via VAE. (b) We expand pretrained video DiT to incorporate hand-pose conditioning using distribution-aligned patch embedding branch. (c) The model is distilled into causal student for interactive, autoregressive generation using streaming rollout schedule. where [0, 1]. The network vΘ is trained to predict the velocity field, conditioned on the reference-image latent zref = E(Iref ) and our depth-aware skeletal control latent c: LCFM = Et,z0,ϵ (cid:104) vΘ(zt, t, zref , c) (ϵ z0)2 2 (cid:105) (2) During inference, the video is generated by solving the ODE defined by vΘ using distilled causal student for real-time responsiveness.

sections.title: 3.2 Depth-Aware Action Representation
sections.content: We represent actions as sequence of skeletal videos derived from 21-joint hand tracking. To resolve the inherent depth ambiguity in standard 2D projectionswhich is critical for modeling precise hand-object interactionswe employ depth-modulated rendering technique. Specifically, the depth-encoded color mapping and diameter of each joint and bone are dynamically scaled according to their camera-space depth (as illustrated in Fig. 2). This representation effectively encapsulates articulated hand structures alongside spatial cues essential for manipulation. To ensure the control signal is compatible with the video generation pipeline, the rendered pose video is projected into the latent space using pretrained VAE encoder. This yields control latent RCT HW that is spatially and temporally aligned with the target video latent, facilitating fine-grained grounding between action and appearance.

sections.title: 3.3 Action-Conditioned Video Generation
sections.content: We build our world model upon Video Diffusion Transformer (DiT) (Wang et al., 2025a), conditioning the generation process on hand-pose sequences as high-dimensional proxy for physical actions. Pose-to-Video Conditioning. Our model follows an image-to-video paradigm: given the reference-image latent zref and control latent c, the model predicts the subsequent motion. To integrate the pose information, we adopt an additive patch-embedding scheme with distribution alignment. We introduce dedicated control patch-embedding layer PatchEmbedc . Their outputs are fused via learnable scalar gate α: in parallel to the original video embedding layer PatchEmbedz CD CD = PatchEmbedz µc σc where zt RCT HW is the noisy video latent and (cid:101)c is the aligned control latent. Since and originate from different modalities, they exhibit distinct statistics. We maintain running estimates of the mean and standard deviation (µ, σ) for both signals and align to the video latent distribution before patchification. CD(zt) + α PatchEmbedc CD((cid:101)c) , σz + µz, (cid:101)c = (3) Table 1 Statistics of the datasets used for egocentric human pretraining and robotic domain adaptation. We report the total number of frames and the resulting video slices after temporal segmentation. Dataset Type Seq. Length Total Frames Total Slices Egocentric Human Pretraining VITRA (Li et al., 2025c) Human EgoDex (Hoque et al., 2025) Human Robotic Domain Adaptation Ours (Real-Robot) Robot 25 81 81 30.7M 74.0M 1.23M 0.91M 0.43M 5.3K This ensures the additive control signal remains statistically compatible with the pretrained video stream throughout training. To preserve the generative prior, PatchEmbedc is zero-initialized and the gating scalar α is initialized to small value (e.g., 0.1), allowing the network to gradually incorporate the pose signal without destabilizing the pretrained weights.

sections.title: 3.4 Progressive Cross-Domain Training
sections.content: To facilitate the transfer of dexterous manipulation skills from human priors to robotic embodiments, we adopt two-stage training paradigm. To bridge the humanrobot embodiment gap between these two training stages, we convert all data into paired (2D Skeleton, RGB) sequences, and present the per-stage training data statistics in Tab. 1. Stage 1: Egocentric Human Pretraining. The model is first trained on large-scale egocentric human video datasets. In this stage, the DiT learns the fundamental dynamics of hand-object interactions and establishes the mapping from skeletal configurations to realistic visual synthesis. We utilize EgoDex (Hoque et al., 2025) and VITRA (Li et al., 2025c) for pre-training. Specifically, EgoDex is segmented into 81-frame clips. To generate the skeleton guidance, we transform the 3D SE(3) hand poses into the camera frame and project them into 2D space to render joint-link skeleton videos, encompassing both hands and forearms. For VITRA, we extract 25-frame segments and utilize the provided hand action annotations (21 joints in MediaPipe format). These joints are projected into 2D skeleton videos using the camera intrinsics and the camera pose of each clips initial frame to maintain spatial grounding. Stage 2: Robotic Domain Adaptation. We fine-tune the model on paired teleoperation data, where human gestures are mapped to robotic actions via Inverse Kinematics (IK). This allows the model to function as robot-centric world model, where human-like intent directly drives robotic execution videos. To provide the necessary diverse physical interactions for training RynnWorld-Teleop, we collected large-scale real-world dataset focused on complex bimanual coordination. This dataset consists of four distinct tasks designed to challenge the models understanding of object dynamics and long-horizon manipulation. To capture human-centric motion priors effectively, we record the operators hand movements using motion capture (MoCap) system, which provides synchronized 2D skeleton sequences. To ensure temporal consistency with the pre-training phase, all robot trajectories are segmented into 81-frame clips. This unified skeleton-based representation, combined with consistent temporal alignment, facilitates an effective transfer of manipulation capabilities from human demonstrations to robotic embodiments. In total, we collected 1,800 high-quality demonstration episodes. Unlike the task-specific policy training, all 1,800 episodes are utilized to train RynnWorld-Teleop, allowing the generative model to learn comprehensive world prior from broad range of physical scenarios. The specific tasks and their respective data distributions are detailed in Table 2.

sections.title: 3.5 Autoregressive Distillation
sections.content: To support interactive, closed-loop applications, we distill the bidirectional teacher model into causal student model (Huang et al., 2025) capable of generating videos autoregressively at the frame level. The process involves reconfiguring the model for streaming inference, followed by two-stage optimization: causal flow-matching warm-up and an adversarial distribution matching stage. Table 2 Statistics of the real-world dataset used for RynnWorld-Teleop training. Task Name Dual Picking Block Pushing Description Pick an apple and banana from plate to the table. Push block from left to center, and then to the right using the right hand. Number 500 500 Bimanual Lifting Lift watermelon pillow with both hands and place Lid Placement Total it into tray. Place the lid of cardboard box onto the box. 500 300 1, Causal Streaming Architecture. The student model employs causal temporal mask and fixed-size KV cache (a pre-allocated buffer with in-place writes) to enable low-latency inference. Unlike the teacher, the students attention at time is restricted to {1, . . . , t}. To maintain long-term consistency during autoregressive rollouts, we retain the embedding of Iref as persistent sink token and append all subsequently generated KV states to the cache. This design preserves the identity and spatial context of the initial reference image throughout generation. Causal Flow-Matching Warm-up. The training begins with causal flow-matching (Lipman et al., 2022) objective to bridge the gap between bidirectional and causal processing. We train the student to predict the velocity field vθ in frame-causal manner by minimizing the mean squared error: LMSE = Et,ϵ vθ(xt, t) (ϵ x0)2(cid:105) (cid:104) . (4) This phase establishes the students streaming generation and control-following capabilities using single-step velocity regression, providing stable initialization for sampling acceleration. Distribution Matching Distillation. To achieve high-fidelity synthesis in only four denoising steps, we subsequently employ Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) (Yin et al., 2024). This refinement utilizes learned critic and the frozen teacher to provide score-based gradient guidance, pushing the students output distribution toward that of the teacher. During this phase, the student performs 4-step sampling rollouts. Crucially, we backpropagate gradients through the persisted KV cache across successive chunks. This cross-chunk gradient flow encourages the model to minimize boundary artifacts at transitions, ensuring smooth temporal continuity and enabling the final model to match the visual quality of the teacher in real-time, interactive loop.

sections.title: 4 RynnWorld-Teleop as a Digital Teleoperation System
sections.content: Sec. 3 establishes RynnWorld-Teleop as an action-conditioned egocentric world model that turns hand-pose stream into high-fidelity, real-time robotic video. To realize the promise of digital teleoperation, however, the world model alone is not enough: it must be embedded in closed loop that captures operator intent, drives the generator, and produces trajectories directly consumable by downstream policy. In this section, we describe the surrounding system that turns RynnWorld-Teleop into such data engine. Sec. 4.1 details the end-to-end pipeline that converts raw operator motion into paired (video, action) trajectories for imitation learning, while Sec. 4.2 contrasts this paradigm with traditional physical simulation and clarifies the unique advantages of digital teleoperation as scalable data source.

sections.title: 4.1 Data Generation Pipeline for Policy Learning
sections.content: To facilitate scalable policy training, we develop systematic pipeline to generate synthetic robot trajectories by leveraging RynnWorld-Teleop as high-fidelity data engine. Retargeting. Given the raw 6-DoF poses from the Vive trackers worn on the operators chest, wrists, and upper arms, we first compute each arms target end-effector pose via calibrated coordinate-transform chain that maps the operators workspace to the robots workspace through translational scaling factor. The corresponding joint configuration is then obtained with an iterative damped least-squares (DLS) inversekinematics solver, regularized by null-space shoulder prior derived from the upper-arm trackers to keep the arm in natural, human-like configurations. Joint limits are enforced after each update. This yields, for every operator gesture, synchronized 54-dimensional robot action vector (dual 7-DoF arms + dual 20-DoF dexterous hands) that is exactly aligned with the hand-pose stream driving RynnWorld-Teleop. The full transform chain, DLS formulation, and null-space objective are detailed in Appendix B. Skeletal-Conditioned Synthesis. For each source demonstration, we extract the initial RGB frame as the reference image Iref . The operators hand-pose stream is rendered into 2D depth-aware skeletal sequence S1:T at 16 FPS. By conditioning RynnWorld-Teleop on Iref and S1:T , we synthesize the corresponding robotic execution video. Notably, because Iref is the sole visual specification of the target scene, it can also be user-provided or synthetically edited to instantiate manipulation scenes beyond the training distribution. We validate this generalization capability in Sec. 5.3. In either case, the ground-truth robotic actions a1:T are synchronized with the generated video frames. Each action at R54 is concatenated vector of the absolute joint positions for the dual 7-DoF robotic arms and dual 20-DoF dexterous hands. These actions are retargeted from the operators poses during the original teleoperation session, ensuring that synthesized visual observations and robotic states are perfectly aligned for downstream imitation learning. Mitigating Drift via Chunked Re-anchoring. Generating long-horizon videos in purely autoregressive manner can lead to visual drift and cumulative physical inconsistencies. To ensure the synthetic data remains physically grounded, we adopt chunk-based generation strategy with segment length of 81 frames. For the first chunk, the model is initialized with the true starting frame of the demo. For each subsequent chunk, we re-anchor the generation by providing the actual egocentric frame from the robots camera at that specific timestep as the new Iref . This periodic grounding ensures that the synthesized environment (e.g., object poses and lighting) remains consistent with the ground-truth action sequence throughout complex, long-horizon tasks.

sections.title: 4.2 Data Engine Comparison with Traditional Simulation
sections.content: We compare RynnWorld-Teleop with traditional physical simulators to highlight its advantages as scalable data engine: Zero 3D Asset Overhead: Traditional simulation requires explicit 3D meshes and URDFs for every object (Guo et al., 2026; Zhao et al., 2025b). RynnWorld-Teleop instantiates the environment implicitly from single reference image Iref , completely bypassing the manual asset modeling bottleneck. No Visual Domain Gap: Simulated rendering inherently suffers from reality gap (Zhao et al., 2026b). RynnWorld-Teleop synthesizes frames directly within the real-world pixel distribution, naturally capturing complex lighting, reflections, and sensor noise without domain randomization. Implicit Physics vs. Manual SysID: Simulators require tedious System Identification (friction, mass) and struggle with deformable objects. By pre-training on large-scale human videos, RynnWorld-Teleop absorbs physical common sense, generating plausible contact dynamics without explicit differential equations.

sections.title: 5.1 Training Details
sections.content: We adopt Wan2.2-TI2V-5B (Wang et al., 2025a) as our base model. To align the base model with the visual characteristics of our target environments (e.g., lighting, specific objects), we first perform standard text-and-image-to-video (TI2V) fine-tuning as warm-up. This stage does not involve action conditioning. We train the full model for 2,000 steps with learning rate of 2 105 on 64 NVIDIA H100 GPUs, allowing the model to adapt to the specific visual domain of the human and robotic datasets. Action-Conditioned Training. We introduce the depth-aware skeletal conditioning branch and explore two training paradigms to adapt the model: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and full-parameter Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT). Both paradigms follow two-stage progressive schedule: Stage 1 (Egocentric Human Pretraining): We initialize the model with the pretrained Wan-2.2-TI2V-5B backbone and learn fundamental manipulation dynamics on large-scale human datasets. Figure 4 Task illustration. We design four manipulation tasks for real-world evaluation. LoRA-based: We optimize the patch embedding layers and LoRA weights (rank 64) with learning rate of 2 105. Full-parameter SFT: We perform full-parameter fine-tuning with learning rate of 2 105, employing 200-step warmup and EMA (decay 0.999) to stabilize the learning of interaction priors. Stage 2 (Robotic Domain Adaptation): We fine-tune the Stage 1 checkpoints on 1,800 paired human-robot episodes to bridge the embodiment gap. LoRA-based: We continue training the LoRA weights and control branch with learning rate of 1 105. Full-parameter SFT: We refine the full model parameters with learning rate of 1 105, incorporating EMA (decay 0.999) and 200-step warm-up to ensure stability across complex bimanual coordination tasks. Autoregressive Distillation. Finally, the bidirectional teacher is converted into causal student for real-time inference: Causal Flow-Matching Warm-up: The student model undergoes warm-up phase to establish temporal causality with learning rate of 1 105. Adversarial Distillation (DMD): For few-step interactive generation, we apply DMD with generative learning rate of 2 106 and critic learning rate of 5 107.

sections.title:
5.2 Digital Teleoperation System Evaluation
Experimental Setup. Experiments are conducted on a TIANJI M6 mobile robot equipped with dual arms
and dual WUJI dexterous hands. An egocentric RealSense D435i captures the visual stream. Please refer to
Appendix A for hardware and the inverse kinematics solver details.
sections.content: As shown in Fig. 4, to demonstrate generalization across diverse manipulation challenges, we evaluate our method on four distinct tasks requiring varying levels of bimanual coordination: (1) Dual Picking: The robot uses its left arm to pick an apple and its right arm to pick banana from plate, sequentially placing both objects onto the tabletop. (2) Block Pushing: sequential pushing task where the left arm pushes large block from the left zone to the center, after which the right arm takes over to push it to the designated right target zone. (3) Bimanual Lifting: heavy-load coordination task where both arms must synchronously lift watermelon plush from the table and accurately place it into tray. (4) Lid Placement: precision-oriented task requiring the robot to pick up lid and accurately align it to cover cardboard box. These tasks collectively challenge the models proficiency in dual-arm synergy, temporal sequencing, and long-horizon interaction. For each task, we evaluate the models generalization by applying significant randomization to the initial environment state. This includes varying the 6-DoF poses of task-relevant objects (e.g., fruits, containers) in terms of both workspace coordinates and axial rotations. The primary evaluation Table 3 Real-world policy performance. Success rates (%) across four complex tasks. Method Data Source Dual Picking Block Pushing Bimanual Lifting Lid Placement DP (Chi et al., 2025) DP (Chi et al., 2025) π0.5 (Intelligence et al., 2025) π0.5 (Intelligence et al., 2025) π0 (Black et al., 2024) π0 (Black et al., 2024) π0 (Black et al., 2024) 300 Real 300 Real + 300 RynnWorld-Teleop 300 Real 300 Real + 300 RynnWorld-Teleop 300 Real 0 Real + 300 RynnWorld-Teleop 300 Real + 300 RynnWorld-Teleop 82.86 88.57 94.29 97.14 88.57 68.57 94.29 85.71 88.57 100.00 97.14 94.29 82.86 100.00 88.57 94.29 94.29 100.00 91.43 77.14 97.14 57.14 65.71 42.86 62.86 34.29 28.57 54.29 Figure 5 Feature Distribution Analysis via t-SNE. We visualize the high-dimensional feature embeddings of real-world trajectories and RynnWorld-Teleop-generated trajectories. metric is the Success Rate, defined as the percentage of successful completions over 35 consecutive real-world trials per task. trial is considered successful if the robot reaches the target state within 120 seconds. Policy Learning via Generative Data Scaling. Beyond visual fidelity, RynnWorld-Teleop functions as generative data engine for digital teleoperation: it scales robot training sets by translating unconstrained human gestures into photorealistic execution trajectories that are perfectly synchronized with ground-truth actions. We evaluate this capability by augmenting three state-of-the-art policies: Diffusion Policy (DP) (Chi et al., 2025), π0.5 (Intelligence et al., 2025), and π0 (Black et al., 2024). As shown in Tab. 3, augmenting 300 real-world episodes with 300 RynnWorld-Teleop-generated episodes leads to consistent performance gains across nearly all tasks. The improvement is most pronounced in high-precision tasks like Lid Placement, where the success rate of π0.5 increases from 42.86% to 62.86% (+20%) and π0 from 34.29% to 54.29% (+20%). Notably, π0 trained solely on 300 RynnWorld-Teleop-generated episodes (without any real data) achieves competitive success rate of 82.86% in Block Pushing and 77.14% in Bimanual Lifting. This Zero-Real-Data performance highlights that our models synthesized videos are not only visually realistic but also physically grounded, capturing the essential dynamics of dexterous manipulation. By transforming low-cost human gesture sequences into high-value robotic training data, RynnWorld-Teleop provides scalable solution to the data scarcity bottleneck in real-world robotics. Feature Distribution Analysis. To further investigate the visual and semantic alignment between real-world robot trajectories and those synthesized by RynnWorld-Teleop, we perform feature distribution analysis using t-SNE. We randomly sample 1000 frames each from our real-world demonstration set and the RynnWorldTeleop-generated dataset. Features are extracted using pre-trained I3D (Carreira and Zisserman, 2017) to capture high-level semantic information. As shown in Fig. 5, the distribution of RynnWorld-Teleop-generated data significantly overlaps with the realworld data. This high degree of alignment indicates that our generative world model successfully captures the underlying distribution of robotic manipulation, including complex hand-object interactions and environmental lighting. This feature-level consistency explains the effective Zero-shot Sim2Real transfer observed in our policy learning experiments. Table 4 Quantitative results of RynnWorld-Teleop. We report visual quality metrics and inference speeds. Textand Action-conditioned World Models, as well as the Ablation Study, are evaluated on EgoDex-Test, while Robot-Specific evaluations are conducted on Robotic-Test. Method PSNR SSIM LPIPS FVD FPS CogVideoX-1.5-I2V-5B (Yang et al., 2024) Wan-2.2-TI2V-5B (Wang et al., 2025a) Wan-2.1-I2V-14B (Wang et al., 2025a) Wan-2.2-I2V-14B (Wang et al., 2025a) Wan-2.2-TI2V-5B (SFT) (Wang et al., 2025a) Text-conditioned World Models 18.22 18.61 18.08 21.05 20.93 0.786 0.772 0.735 0.816 0.806 InterDyn (Akkerman et al., 2025) CosHand (Sudhakar et al., 2024) Mask2IV (Li et al., 2026a) RynnWorld-Teleop (LoRA (Hu et al., 2022)) RynnWorld-Teleop (SFT) RynnWorld-Teleop-Causal Action-conditioned World Models 0.831 21.47 0.785 18.14 0.836 21.50 0.876 26.08 0.887 26.78 0.830 22.25 Concatenation Fusion w/o Human Pre-training w/o DMD (Causal) w/o Causal Warm-up (Causal) Ablation Study (LoRA) 19.69 17.81 19.25 14.26 0.821 0.763 0.777 0.688 0.322 0.373 0.418 0.265 0.282 0.279 0.406 0.219 0.151 0.119 0.207 0.260 0.453 0.244 0.408 RynnWorld-Teleop RynnWorld-Teleop-Causal Action-conditioned Robot-Specific World Models 22.53 18.66 0.898 0.743 0.148 0.249 2790 1998 1540 1337 1223 655 1527 1650 585 550 1226 1191 2598 1338 763 1534 0.8 2.8 0.3 0.3 2.8 2.9 0.8 0.9 2.8 2.8 40.0 2.8 2.8 40.0 40.0 2.8 40.0 Latency Analysis for Real-time Control. To validate the real-time capability of RynnWorld-Teleop (Causal), we evaluate its end-to-end inference latency on single NVIDIA H100 GPU. Our distilled causal student model, optimized with 4-step flow matching schedule, achieves high throughput of 40.0 fps at 480 832 resolution. The average per-frame latency is approximately 25 ms, which can be decomposed into three primary stages: Skeletal Action Encoding (5%): Pre-processing and VAE encoding of the depth-aware skeletal sequence to provide the conditioning latent. Causal DiT Denoising (72%): Autoregressive latent generation via 4-step DiT inference, leveraging sliding-window KV cache for temporal efficiency. Visual Decoding (23%): VAE decoding from the latent space to the final RGB pixel space. This performance results in an effective interactive frequency of 40 Hz, which significantly exceeds the typical 210 Hz frame rates of existing action-conditioned world models Wang et al. (2026); Akkerman et al. (2025). Crucially, this throughput matches or exceeds the standard 30 Hz frequency of real-world robotic cameras, thereby minimizing the sensing-to-actuation gap and ensuring fluid, responsive experience during digital teleoperation.

sections.title:
5.3 Action-Conditioned World Model Evaluation
Experimental Setup. To provide a comprehensive assessment of RynnWorld-Teleop’s generative capabilities,
we establish two distinct evaluation benchmarks as reported in Tab. 4:
sections.content: EgoDex-Test (Human-Centric Domain): For evaluating general interaction priors and conducting the ablation study, we utilize the EgoDex dataset (Hoque et al., 2025). We randomly sample 50 video sequences from the official EgoDex test set, each consisting of 81 frames at 16 FPS. These sequences cover wide variety of unseen human-object interactions. Using this representative subset ensures an unbiased evaluation of how well the model transfers large-scale human manipulation priors to action-conditioned synthesis. Figure 6 Qualitative Results of RynnWorld-Teleop. Our model effectively maps single hand-gesture stream to both human and robotic embodiments. By pre-training on large-scale egocentric human videos, RynnWorld-Teleop absorbs rich manipulation priors and successfully transfers this knowledge to robotic execution. The synthesized videos maintain high fidelity and temporal coherence. Robotic-Test (Robot-Specific Domain): To assess the models performance on actual robotic embodiments and real-world laboratory environments, we hold out specific Robotic-Test set. This set consists of 20 video sequences (81 frames each) randomly selected from our self-collected teleoperation data, ensuring they were not seen during the Stage 2 Robotic Adaptation training. These sequences encompass all four task categories (Dual Picking, Block Pushing, Bimanual Lifting, and Lid Placement) to verify that RynnWorld-Teleop can maintain high visual fidelity and temporal consistency across diverse robotic manipulation scenarios. To evaluate action-conditioned video generation, we employ comprehensive set of metrics: PSNR and SSIM for structural fidelity, LPIPS for perceptual similarity, and FVD to assess temporal coherence. For FVD, we use the standard I3D backbone to evaluate the distribution alignment and temporal coherence of the 81-frame rollouts. All metrics are averaged across the respective test sequences to ensure statistical significance. We also report FPS measured on single NVIDIA H100 GPU. Action-Conditioned World Modeling. As shown in Tab. 4, our method significantly outperforms both generalpurpose I2V models and action conditioned video generation models. General I2V baselines like Wan (Wang et al., 2025a) and CogVideoX (Yang et al., 2024), while possessing strong texture priors, lack the mechanism to respond to fine-grained hand gestures, leading to poor temporal coherence in manipulation (FVD > 1300). Notably, vanilla SFT baseline that directly fine-tunes Wan-2.2-TI2V5B on the same robotic and human datasets (denoted as SFT in Tab. 4) still trails RynnWorld-Teleop by wide margin (FVD 1223 vs. 585; PSNR 20.93 vs. 26.08). This comparison underscores that simple supervised fine-tuning is insufficient for mastering complex robotic dexterity. In contrast, RynnWorld-Teleops superior performance stems from our specialized depth-aware pose representation and distribution-aligned conditioning, which Figure 7 Generalization to Out-of-Distribution (OOD) Visual States. RynnWorld-Teleop generalizes along two axes that are absent from training: (i) unseen objects (top two rows), where the manipulated item in the reference frame is replaced by novel category or shape; and (ii) unseen backgrounds (bottom two rows), where the tabletop texture is swapped for an environment not seen during training. In all cases, the modified reference image is obtained via off-the-shelf image editing, without any real-world object or scene modification. RynnWorld-Teleop preserves high visual fidelity, temporal coherence, and physically plausible dynamics under both object-level and background-level shifts. Figure 8 Conditioning Strategy. Compared to concatenation, our distribution-aligned additive scheme (Eq. 3) preserves pre-trained weights, ensuring stable synthesis. Figure 9 Effect of Pretraining. Stage 1 pretraining provides robust interaction prior. Without it, the model fails to maintain visual quality when fine-tuned on limited robotic data. provide more explicit and effective action grounding. Compared to human-centric models like CosHand (Sudhakar et al., 2024) and Mask2IV (Li et al., 2026a), RynnWorld-Teleop effectively bridges the embodiment gap by accurately rendering the complex robotic embodiments. Our distilled Causal student model maintains this high fidelity while supporting interactive generation, facilitating responsive closed-loop applications, supported by the visualizations in Fig. 6. Generalization to Out-of-Distribution (OOD) Visual States. key advantage of digital teleoperation is that new manipulation scene can be instantiated from single reference image. To validate this claim, beyond the closed-set benchmarks above, we assess RynnWorld-Teleops ability to generalize to scenes that lie outside the training distribution. Specifically, we consider two complementary axes of visual OOD generalization: unseen objects and unseen backgrounds. For unseen objects, we replace the manipulated item in the reference image with category or shape that never appears in the training data (e.g., substituting wooden block with red sphere, or watermelon plush with rugby ball). For unseen backgrounds, we swap the tabletop texture in the reference image (e.g., overlaying patterned tablecloth on plain white table). In both settings, the modified reference image is produced via off-the-shelf image editing, without any real-world object or scene modification. As shown in Fig. 7, RynnWorld-Teleop maintains temporal consistency and faithfully executes the action-conditioned trajectory under both object-level and background-level shifts. These results suggest that RynnWorld-Teleop learns generalized interaction prior that is decoupled from specific object textures and background appearances, supporting the paradigm-level claim that digital teleoperation can instantiate arbitrary target scenes from single reference image.

sections.title:
5.4 Ablation Study
Conditioning Strategy: Addition vs. Concatenation. We investigate the impact of different fusion strategies for
integrating pose control. We compare our distribution-aligned additive scheme (Eq. 3) with the standard
Concatenation Fusion, where control latents are appended to the noisy video latents. As shown in Tab. 4,
Concat Fusion results in a significant performance drop, with FVD increasing from 585 to 1191. This indicates
that direct concatenation disrupts the pre-trained latent distribution of the base DiT, leading to unstable
synthesis. Qualitatively, as shown in Fig. 8, our additive approach with zero-initialized gating effectively
preserves the generative priors, ensuring better visual fidelity while maintaining precise control.
sections.content: Value of Human Egocentric Pre-training. To validate our two-stage training paradigm, we evaluate variant w/o Human Pre-training, which is directly fine-tuned on robotic data. Quantitative results in Tab. 4 show severe performance collapse (FVD 2598, LPIPS 0.453), as the limited robotic data is insufficient to teach the model complex hand-object physics from scratch. As visualized in Fig. 9, without the interaction priors from human videos, the model fails to synthesize realistic hand structures and exhibits severe ghosting and blurred textures, ultimately leading to loss of object permanence where the robot effector and the target apple vanish entirely during manipulation. This confirms that transferring knowledge from large-scale human egocentric datasets is crucial for bridging the embodiment gap and achieving high-quality robotic world modeling. Effectiveness of Sequential Distillation. We evaluate the necessity of our two-stage distillation curriculum in Tab. 4. Comparing the final model with the Flow-Matching-only baseline (w/o DMD) reveals significant quality gap (PSNR 22.25 vs. 19.25). While flow-matching establishes the basic causal architecture, it lacks the high-fidelity refinement of the teacher. More importantly, we observe that directly applying DMD (Yin et al., 2024) to the bidirectional teacher without the causal warm-up phase (w/o Causal Warm-up) leads to severe training instability and blurred textures. This confirms that the first stage (Causal Warm-up) is essential for bridging the structural gap between bidirectional and causal processing, providing stable initialization that allows the subsequent DMD (Yin et al., 2024) phase to successfully inherit the teachers complex hand-object interaction priors in just 4 steps.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: In this paper, we presented RynnWorld-Teleop, generative digital teleoperation framework that bridges the gap between unconstrained human gestures and precise robotic execution videos. By introducing depth-aware hand pose representation and progressive two-stage training paradigm, our model successfully transfers interaction priors from large-scale human datasets to specific robotic embodiments. To support real-time interactive use cases, we further distilled the bidirectional teacher model into causal student via novel streaming autoregressive distillation procedure, ensuring spatiotemporal consistency over extended horizons. Experimental results demonstrate that RynnWorld-Teleop not only synthesizes high-fidelity, action-consistent interaction videos but also serves as powerful generative data engine. Augmenting real-world robotic datasets with trajectories synthesized by RynnWorld-Teleop significantly improves the success rate and generalization of manipulation policies. We believe this work provides scalable path toward training general-purpose robotic agents by leveraging human intuition as digital control signal, and we hope digital teleoperation will become standard ingredient in scalable robot data pipelines. Limitation. While RynnWorld-Teleop successfully demonstrates digital teleoperation as viable data engine, several limitations remain. First, while the depth-modulated rendering captures 3D spatial dynamics, the model occasionally struggles with complex physical phenomena such as fine-grained liquid dynamics or the manipulation of highly deformable objects. Addressing these cases will likely require richer training data covering such interactions. Second, bridging the embodiment gap currently requires per-platform fine-tuning, which limits the paradigms scalability across robot fleets. We view cross-embodiment foundation world models, possibly conditioned on robot kinematic descriptors, as promising direction.

The evaluation utilizes our standard robotic setup: bimanual TIANJI M6 system equipped with WUJI hands. Detailed specifications including degrees of freedom (DoF), payload, and fingertip force are summarized to ensure the reproducibility of our manipulation benchmark.

sections.title: A Real Robot System Setup
sections.content: Our real robot is built on the TIANJI M6 and WUJI Hand, as shown in Fig. 10. The policys inference frequency is set at 50 Hz. The commands are sent with delay kept between 18 and 30 milliseconds. The low-level interface operates at frequency of 500 Hz, ensuring smooth real-time control. The communication between the control policy and the low-level interface is realized through LCM (Lightweight Communications and Marshalling). We collect real-world demonstration data through teleoperation system. Our hardware setup consists of dual TIANJI 7-DoF robotic arms and dual WUJI 20-DoF dexterous hands, yielding total of 54 degrees of freedom. For arm control, the human operator wears five HTC Vive trackers (mounted on the chest, both wrists, and both upper arms). The system computes wrist-to-chest relative transforms at 100-120 Hz and feeds them into Pinocchio-based inverse kinematics solver running in separate process. The resulting joint commands are further smoothed by Ruckig trajectory generator with velocity, acceleration, and jerk constraints before being sent to the robot arms at 200 Hz. For hand control, the operator, as shown in Fig. 11, wears Manus data gloves. The raw glove signals are converted to 21-point MediaPipe hand skeleton format and retargeted to the 20-DoF WUJI hand joint space via dedicated retargeting module, with an exponential moving average filter applied for motion smoothing. Retargeting in RynnWorld-Teleop We provide here the full retargeting pipeline summarized in Sec. 4.1. Given the raw poses from the Vive trackers, we first compute the target end-effector pose for each arm via coordinate transform chain: Ttarget = Tbase Scale(T chest Twrist) Tee, (5) where T1 chest Twrist extracts the wrist-to-chest relative pose in tracker space, the Scale() operator scales only the translation component by factor (e.g., = 1.5) to map the operators workspace to the robots Figure 11 Operator setup for real-world data collection. workspace, and Tbase, Tee are fixed calibration transforms that align the tracker coordinate frame with the robot base frame and end-effector frame, respectively. We solve the inverse kinematics using an iterative damped least-squares (DLS) method. At each iteration, the 6D task-space error R6 (position and orientation) is computed, and the joint update is: where J# λ is the damped pseudo-inverse obtained via SVD: = J# λ e, = UΣV, J# λ = diag (cid:19) (cid:18) σi σ2 + λ2 U, (6) (7) with an adaptive damping factor λ = λmin + 0.01 1+σmax To produce natural arm configurations, we exploit null-space shoulder prior derived from the upper-arm trackers. Specifically, the arm tracker position is transformed into the robot frame to yield reference elbow position, from which we solve partial IK (least-squares on 3 2 sub-Jacobian of joints q0, q1) to obtain shoulder reference values qref . These are injected as weighted null-space task: that increases near singularities. shoulder where = 0.5 for the shoulder joints. Joint limits are enforced by hard clipping after each integration step. q + (I J# λ J) (qref q), (8)

sections.title: C Visual Comparison Results
sections.content: As shown in Fig. 12, RynnWorld-Teleop produces more temporally stable and physically plausible interactions compared to mask-based methods. While methods like Mask2IV maintain structural shape, they often suffer from texture flickering and lack fine-grained finger articulation. In contrast, our depth-aware skeletal representation provides explicit structural constraints, allowing RynnWorld-Teleop to synthesize realistic finger-object contact even in complex bimanual scenarios on the EgoDex (Hoque et al., 2025) benchmark. Figure 12 Qualitative comparison. Unlike baselines that rely on SAM-generated masks for conditioning, RynnWorldTeleop uses sparse skeletal poses, achieving superior fidelity and temporal coherence. Note that baselines are visualized at their native supported resolutions. Figure 13 Qualitative results of RynnWorld-Teleop on robotic manipulation tasks. Starting from single reference image and sequence of human hand-pose streams, RynnWorld-Teleop synthesizes high-fidelity, temporally coherent robotic execution videos. The results demonstrate the models ability to render complex dexterous interactions, such as bimanual coordination and high-precision object handling, while maintaining strict adherence to the input action signal. Furthermore, we demonstrate the practical efficacy of RynnWorld-Teleop in the target robotic domain in Fig. 13. By successfully transferring interaction priors from human datasets to the WUJI dexterous hand, our model synthesizes photorealistic execution videos at high resolution. These results show that RynnWorldTeleop accurately captures the intricate mechanical dynamics and environmental reflections, producing robotic trajectories that are visually indistinguishable from real-world teleoperation recordings across diverse tasks such as Lid Placement and Bimanual Lifting.

sections.title: D Baseline Implementation Details
sections.content: CosHand. CosHand (Sudhakar et al., 2024) is diffusion-based hand image generation model that synthesizes target hand appearances conditioned on reference image and target hand masks. We use the officially released checkpoint and adopt its image-conditioned generation pipeline. For each video, we extract frame 0 as the reference image and use SAM2 (Ravi et al., 2025) to obtain per-frame binary hand segmentation masks from the ground truth video. At each timestep > 0, we condition on the reference image, the reference hand mask (frame 0), and the target hand mask (frame t), and generate the target frame at 256 256 resolution using DDIM sampling with 50 steps and classifier-free guidance scale of 1.5. The generated frames are then resized to 480 832 to match the ground truth resolution, producing 81 frames at 16 fps per video. Mask2IV. Mask2IV (Li et al., 2026a) is two-stage latent video diffusion model that generates videos conditioned on an input image, text prompt, and start/end hand masks. We use the officially released checkpoints for both stages. For each ground truth video, we extract SAM2 (Ravi et al., 2025) hand masks and divide the 81-frame sequence into 5 non-overlapping chunks of 16 frames. For chunk i, the conditioning consists of: the ground truth frame at index 16i as the input image, the hand masks at frames 16i (start) and 16(i + 1) (end), and text prompt derived from the task name (e.g., hand pouring). Inference is performed at 320 512 resolution with 50 DDIM steps, η = 1.0, classifier-free guidance scale of 1.0, and guidance rescale of 0.7. The 5 chunks are stitched sequentially to form an 80-frame output video, which is then resized to 480 832 for evaluation. InterDyn. InterDyn (Akkerman et al., 2025) extends Stable Video Diffusion (SVD) with ControlNet branch that accepts hand interaction masks as spatial conditioning. We convert each ground truth video from 16 fps to 12 fps via frame resampling and split it into 2 non-overlapping chunks of 28 frames.The corresponding SAM2 (Ravi et al., 2025) hand masks are similarly resampled and converted to VP9-encoded WebM format as required by InterDyns input pipeline. For each chunk, InterDyn takes the first frame as the reference image and the 28-frame mask sequence as ControlNet conditioning, producing 14 output frames at 6 fps (temporal sub-sampling factor of 2) with 50 denoising steps. The two chunks yield 28 prediction frames per video at native resolution of 256 384, which are upscaled to 480 832 for metric computation against the ground truth.
